title: Air-dry clay wind chime set
subtitle: Six to eight hand-formed clay chimes strung on a slab crossbar
excerpt: A set of six to eight small clay chimes formed by pinching, rolling, and piercing air-dry clay, then strung on jute cord from a flat slab bar. No kiln is needed. PVA sealing gives the finished set enough weather resistance for a covered porch.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on hand-building methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which clay has dried enough to hold its shape firmly but remains damp enough to be carved, joined, or pierced without crumbling
The stage at which all moisture has left the clay body and the piece is at its most fragile before any surface treatment
A creamy mixture of clay and water used as an adhesive to join separate clay pieces together
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying slip before pressing them together to create a strong bond
Unfired clay work that has dried fully but has not been kiln-fired; fragile and soluble in water
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used as a building unit in hand-building

content.text: This project produces a set of six to eight individual clay chimes hung from a flat clay crossbar on jute or cotton cord. All construction uses air-dry clay and hand-building techniques. No kiln is required at any stage. The finished set suits a covered porch or indoor location where it will not be exposed to sustained rain.
Preparation

Knead each portion of clay for two minutes before forming to remove air pockets and ensure consistent texture. Work on a wooden board lightly dusted with cornflour or covered with a cloth to prevent sticking. Prepare a small pot of
slip
by mixing a marble-sized lump of clay with water until the consistency is thick cream. Keep the clay you are not actively using covered with a damp cloth to prevent premature drying.
Construction
Make the crossbar. Roll out a
slab

8 mm thick and cut a strip approximately 250 mm long by 30 mm wide using the fettling knife. This forms the top bar from which the chimes will hang. Set guides (two rulers or similar straight edges) at the same height to keep rolling even.

Pierce eight hanging holes across the crossbar. Use the needle tool to pierce holes at even intervals (approximately 25 mm apart) along the lower edge of the crossbar, each 8 mm from the edge. Pierce two holes near each end of the upper edge for the hanging cord. Widen each hole gently by rotating the needle tool so the cord will pass through when the clay has shrunk slightly on drying.

Form the chime shapes. Work with 30-40 g of clay per chime. Roll some into tapered cylinders (pinch one end thinner), flatten others slightly for a leaf or teardrop shape, and shape others by pinching from a ball into a small vessel form. Aim for a variety of profiles so the chimes sound at slightly different pitches when they knock together. Each finished chime should be 60-90 mm long.

Pierce the top of each chime. While the clay is still soft, use the needle tool to pierce a hole through the top 8 mm of each chime, perpendicular to the length. The hole must be at least 4 mm in diameter at this stage to allow for shrinkage.

Add surface texture while the clay is still soft. Press a pottery stamp, a length of twisted cord, or a leaf into the face of each chime to create an impression. Work quickly before the surface stiffens. Smooth any rough edges with a damp sponge.
Check all holes are clear. Once the chimes reach the
leather-hard
stage, pass the needle tool through each hole again to clear any clay that has collapsed inward. The crossbar holes should also be checked at this stage.
To add a decorative knob or pendant to any chime, use the
score-and-slip
method. Scratch cross-hatch marks into both surfaces with the needle tool. Apply slip to both, press together firmly, and smooth the join.
Drying

Lay all pieces flat on a wooden board and allow them to reach the leather-hard stage at room temperature (typically 12-24 hours, depending on humidity). Avoid placing pieces near a heat source, as rapid drying causes cracking. Turn the chimes over once they are firm enough to handle without deforming so the undersides dry evenly. The crossbar can be propped slightly on a folded cloth to prevent warping along its length. Leave all pieces until fully
bone-dry
before sealing. At the bone-dry stage the clay will feel room temperature even on a warm day and will appear uniformly pale. The
greenware
stage is fragile: handle pieces with care and avoid getting them wet before sealing.
Finishing

Paint each piece if desired before sealing. Acrylic paints work well on bone-dry air-dry clay; allow paint to dry fully (at least one hour) before applying sealer. Dilute PVA glue 50:50 with water and brush two coats over every surface including through the pierced holes using a thin brush. Allow each coat to dry completely before applying the next. PVA sealing significantly improves humidity resistance but does not make the pieces fully waterproof. Thread the cord through the hanging holes and tie knots on either side of each hole to hold the chimes at the desired height. Space the chimes so they will touch one another gently in a light breeze.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. This set is not suitable as a water vessel and should not be left in rain. A covered porch or indoor hanging location is appropriate. If the surface becomes dusty, wipe with a barely damp cloth and allow to dry. Do not soak or submerge any piece. If a chime breaks at the pierced hole, rethread with a heavier cord or replace the individual chime.
Variations

Driftwood crossbar: replace the clay crossbar with a length of driftwood or a straight branch (approximately 30 cm). Drill or burn small holes along its length at the same spacing and thread the chimes directly from the wood.
Botanical impressions: press individual leaves, fern fronds, or seed pods into the face of each soft chime before piercing. This creates detailed plant impressions as decoration.
Monochrome set: paint all chimes in a single neutral tone (raw umber or cream) and seal with matte PVA rather than gloss. This gives the set a more uniform, sculptural look.

rows.problem: Hole collapses or closes during drying
rows.cause: The hole was pierced too small or the clay wall around it was too thin
rows.fix: Re-pierce at the leather-hard stage using the needle tool; widen to at least 4 mm diameter. If the clay is bone-dry, carefully drill through with a hand drill using a fine bit

rows.cause: One face dried faster than the other
rows.fix: At the leather-hard stage, gently press the bar flat on the board and allow it to finish drying under a light weight such as another board

rows.problem: Chime cracks across its body during drying
rows.cause: The piece dried too rapidly or had an air pocket inside
rows.fix: For pieces still at leather-hard, dampen the crack slightly, work a thin line of slip into it, and press the edges together. For bone-dry cracks, fill with undiluted PVA and allow to cure before painting

rows.problem: Cord keeps slipping through the holes
rows.cause: Holes are too large for the cord diameter chosen
rows.fix: Tie a stopper knot (overhand knot) below each hole before threading from above, or replace with a thicker cord

rows.cause: Sealer was applied before paint was fully dry, or the surface was dusty when painted
rows.fix: Lightly sand the flaking area with fine sandpaper, repaint, allow 2-3 hours to dry fully, then re-seal with two PVA coats
